constraints: high-stakes certification; require rigorous, prioritized study plan
weekday hours may be limited; weekends open for longer blocks
final stretch must protect sleep (7-9 hours) and minimize cognitive load
no all-nighters in the final stretch
avoid learning new material in the final week; focus on consolidation, error review, and pacing
limit caffeine after early afternoon; avoid large late-day doses
avoid heavy practice right before the final 24 hours
avoid heavy practice in the final 48 hours
two-week consolidation window: emphasize consolidation and remediation over new content (days 14-8)
weekend morning three-hour session blocks; pacing-focused scheduling and alignment of full-length mocks to a 3-hour weekend slot
handwritten one-page sheet allowed during exam; plan layout, lookup limits, and preparation strategy
no electronic devices, internet access, or calculators during study or exam; one-page sheet only reference
evening-shifts: three nights per week; late-night study unreliable on those days; schedule high-focus tasks on non-evening days
weekday mornings: ~2 hours; weekend blocks longer
speed-to-solve constraint: convert theoretical knowledge into fast, accurate problem solving
decisions: Initial mock weighting: 50/50 split between knowledge questions and hands-on problems; adjust once exact proportions are known.
Pair weekly work blocks with short spaced-rep/fact recall before hands-on applied blocks; untimed learning followed by timed applied sets.
Implement error-logging by mode to separate factual mistakes from applied/process errors for targeted remediation.
Final weeks focus: more full-length, split simulations and timed transition practice; maintain nightly spaced reviews for facts.
Provide a six-week schedule that allocates sessions for both knowledge and hands-on parts and aligns full mocks to the weekend-morning three-hour format.
Updated weighting: knowledge:applied 60/40; allocate more time for knowledge checks and spaced repetition; progressively more timed applied practice.
Plan to design and test a one-page handwritten sheet layout; incorporate its use into practice mocks; validate lookup rules during practice with timed restrictions.
Emphasize speed-focused drills to convert theory into fast, accurate problem solving; allocate time within sessions for tempo practice.
Threshold rule: don’t time a topic until you can solve representative problems untimed correctly 4–5 times in a row. Then introduce timed drills.

Micro‑timing drills: break a full problem into steps and set time targets for each step (e.g., 30s to read & triage, 90s for setup, Xs for computation, 15s for answer check). Train to those sub‑targets.
Timed re‑solve: after an untimed correct solution, immediately re‑solve the same problem under a strict shorter timer to build fluency.
Mixed short sets: do many short (10–30 minute) timed sets that force frequent triage/decision practice rather than only long mocks.
Triage rules & skip strategy: create a quick checklist for deciding when to skip/mark and return; practice applying it so you don’t waste time on a single item.
Error‑type drills: separate careless/timing errors from conceptual errors. For careless/timing, drill speed and checking routines; for conceptual, return to untimed deliberate practice.
Use your one‑page sheet: practice finding items in <5s during timed drills so it speeds you up instead of slowing you down.
Reduce cognitive overhead: standardize abbreviations, templates, and a short mental script you run at problem start to avoid hesitation.
Track metrics: log timed accuracy, average time/question, and time distribution by section to measure progress and guide practice.
Recovery and pacing: practice the morning‑of exam routine and short in‑exam resets (deep breath, 10s checklist) so anxiety doesn’t bleed time.
If you want, I can convert these into a short set of timed drills you can plug into your weekday 2‑hour blocks and weekend sessions.
